title: Allstate accuses Broadcom of auditing it because it quit VMware, CA
gist: Allstate accuses Broadcom of retaliatory license audits after it decided not to renew VMware and CA contracts, highlighting a growing enterprise exodus from VMware and Broadcom's litigious enforcement of its acquired software licenses.

insights.claim: Allstate alleges Broadcom simultaneously and unreasonably initiated four separate audits of its CA and VMware software use specifically because Allstate decided not to renew its contracts.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: License audits as a retention/monetization weapon is a repeatable Broadcom tactic; enterprises weighing VMware exits should price in audit-and-litigation risk.
insights.quote: This case is about VMware's decision to initiate a haphazard audit of Allstate, once it was aware that Allstate did not intend to renew its contracts with VMware or its sister company, CA.

insights.claim: The cases demonstrate Broadcom's litigious side and willingness to battle disgruntled VMware customers in court.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Aggressive litigation is now part of Broadcom's VMware business model, a reputational and switching-cost factor for the customer base.
insights.locator: The cases demonstrate Broadcom's litigious side

insights.claim: Allstate is one of thousands of companies that have migrated or plan to migrate partially or completely away from VMware after Broadcom's takeover, alongside T-Mobile, Tesco, and Western Union.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A large named-customer migration wave is a tailwind for virtualization alternatives (Nutanix, Proxmox, hyperscalers) and a churn risk for Broadcom's VMware revenue.
insights.locator: The statement reveals that Allstate is one of thousands

insights.claim: Allstate claims it fully removed VMware from all devices by September 2025, making it unable to run Broadcom's audit scripts because they depend on live VMware components.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Complete rip-and-replace is achievable at enterprise scale, undercutting the assumption that VMware lock-in is insurmountable.
